step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the ASSESSMENT phase. Focus on gathering basic facts: how long they have been smoking, how much they smoke per day, and what substance. Do NOT ask about triggers or suggest strategies yet. Ask only one simple factual question.

- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

Therapist: Hello, Rachel. I'm so glad you're here to talk about quitting tobacco. I'm committed to helping you overcome your addiction and live a healthier life. Can you tell me a little bit about your tobacco use and what brings you here today?
Patient: Honestly, I don't really see the point of talking about this. I've tried quitting before, and it never works. I just don't think I'm ready to quit right now.

Therapist: I understand that quitting can be tough, especially if you've tried before and it didn't work out. But I want to let you know that you're not alone, and I'm here to support you. Patient: I don't know...I just get really stressed out sometimes, and smoking helps me relax. And my friends all smoke, so it's just easier to keep doing it, you know? Therapist: I understand that stress and social influences can be tough to overcome. And weight gain is a common concern for many people who quit smoking. Patient: Yeah, I knew it was bad for me, but I didn't realize it was that bad.
